text: Our unit presents a robotic ultralow anterior resection combined with a transanal transection and colloenal anastomosis. The patient is a fifty-eight year old female with a body mass index of twenty-three and previous laparoscopic gastric sleeve performed in two thousand eighteen. She was diagnosed with rectal cancer located one centimeter above the anorectal junction. MRI showed a T-one or T-two N-zero tumor and the endoanal ultrasound revealed a U-T-two N-zero. Our center routinely uses the robotic approach for all rectal tumors located in the mid and distal third of the rectum. Thus, the decision to perform a robotic ultralow anterior resection was made at the MDT.

Four 8mm robotic ports are positioned in a straight line, 4cm to the right of a line joining the head of the femur to the costal margin. Additionally, a 10mm assistant port is placed midway between the costal margin and the anterior superior iliac spine.

The surgeon focuses on the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), dividing it just below the duodenojejunal (DJ) flexure. A medial to lateral plane is developed in front of the aortic fascia. Dissection proceeds under the IMV, identifying and separating the pancreas from the colonic splenic flexure. The omentum is released from the transverse colon, with challenges due to adhesions from previous surgery. Dissection continues on the left paracolic side until the splenic flexure is fully mobilized. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is identified and dissected, starting with an incision at the sacral promontory. The total mesorectal excision (TME) plane is opened and followed cranially, with the IMA traced to its origin and divided using hemolock clips.

The surgeon identifies the superior rectal artery to enter the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane posteriorly. Dissection is performed deeply both anteriorly and posteriorly, starting one centimeter above the peritoneal reflection. The dissection progresses sequentially, beginning with posterior dissection, followed by right lateral, anterior, and then left lateral dissection, reaching down to the pelvic floor.

The surgeon divides the posterior midline roughing to allow the rectum to stretch upwards. A digital rectal examination is performed to identify the tumor level. The vagina is identified, and dissection of the rectovaginal septum continues towards the pelvis. The rectum is carefully pulled out of the pelvis using a plastic tube placed around the distal rectum, providing traction for better visualization of the pelvic floor and tumor identification in the distal rectum.

The robotic platform is undocked, and a lone star retractor is placed to enhance exposure for the transanal transection. The surgeon begins the procedure by closing the anus with a post-transducer to ensure a free distal margin, followed by a small elliptical incision above the sphincters. The dissection continues to connect with the abdominal team, preserving the sphincter complex while maintaining pneumoperitoneum.

The intra-abdominal pressure decreases as the specimen is extracted transanally through an Alexis wound protector. An enhancing coloanal anastomosis is performed using interrupted stitches of Tuo Vicryl. A diverting stoma is created, completing the procedure.
